summary: Laparoscopy typically causes various known physical alterations such as CO₂ absorption leading to hypercapnia or respiratory acidosis; however, it does not usually result in an increase in intracranial pressure under usual circumstances unless specific risk factors are already established for a patient with respect to their cranial health status prior to surgery. Thus, among listed options related directly observable effects post-laparotomy, elevated ICp would be uniquely absent due primarily because no direct mechanism exists within typical surgical practice settings designed specifically around maintaining cerebral perfusion pressures stable throughout procedure duration without additional contributing comorbidities influencing outcome trajectories differently across individuals undergoing similar interventions simultaneously but experiencing varying degrees of baseline vulnerability based upon individualized medical histories reviewed
